questionText: Disprove the statement: ``If two triangles have the same area, then they are congruent.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a conditional for all triangles.
steps.explanation: The claim asserts equal area forces congruence; two equal-area triangles that are not congruent disprove it.
We need a single pair of triangles with equal area but different shapes.
Area depends only on the product of base and height, so different base/height pairs can give the same area.
A triangle with base $4$ and height $3$ has area $\tfrac12\times4\times3 = 6$.
A triangle with base $6$ and height $2$ also has area $\tfrac12\times6\times2 = 6$.
Both triangles have area $6$, satisfying the hypothesis.
Their base and height differ, so the triangles have different dimensions and cannot be congruent.
Equal area only fixes the product base$\times$height; the individual measurements — and hence the shapes — can still differ.
Two triangles with bases/heights $(4,3)$ and $(6,2)$ both have area $6$ but are not congruent, so the claim is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: A triangle with base $4$, height $3$ and one with base $6$, height $2$ both have area $6$, yet they are not congruent. Hence the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
